bird.name: 灰頸岩鶥
bird.summary: The grey-necked rockfowl (Picathartes oreas) is a medium-sized bird in the family Picathartidae with a long neck and tail. Also known as th…

locations.name: 比奥科岛
locations.summary: 比奥科岛（Bioko），原名“费尔南多波岛”（西班牙語：Fernando Poo），是非洲西海岸几内亚湾中的一个火山岛，属赤道几内亚管辖，该国前首都马拉博即位于岛上。该岛面积为2,017平方公里，人口33万人。該島嶼原為西班牙殖民地，因其…

locations.name: 几内亚湾
locations.summary: 几内亚湾（英語：Gulf of Guinea；法語：Golfe de Guinée；葡萄牙語：Golfo da Guiné；西班牙語：Golfo de Guinea）位於非洲西岸，它包括贝宁湾和邦尼湾，是大西洋的一部分。赤道與本初子午線在…

relations.subject: 灰頸岩鶥
relations.evidence: thumb|alt=Mount Cameroon emerges through the clouds in the background, while the foreground depicts dense rainforest|The forests around Mount Cameroon are a nesting area The grey-necked rockfowl is found in West Africa from southeast Nigeria to southwest Gabon. In Nigeria, it is only found in the nation's southeastern corner near the coast and the Cameroon border. The species is widespread in southwestern Cameroon, and this country is considered to be the species' stronghold. It is found throughout Equatorial Guinea and into southwestern Gabon. Additionally, the species resides in the southwestern forests of the island of Bioko in the Gulf of Guinea. There is only one record of this species from the Republic of the Congo, though it is suspected that the rockfowl may have an undiscovered population in this country. The grey-necked rockfowl's total range covers approximately . The grey-necked rockfowl prefers rugged terrain in these forests covered in large boulders, caves, and gorges. Additionally, it often found near inselbergs and a source of water, either a river or a forest pool. The understory of its forests has sparse undergrowth or open spaces but is covered in mosses, ferns, lianas, and epiphytes. In southwestern Bioko it is found in low forests that receive nearly of rain a year. Bioko's habitat also has dense undergrowth and vertical gorges near a caldera. The rockfowl's habitat is normally found between above sea level, though it is lower in elevation on Bioko. This species is non-migratory, and at one site in Cameroon the birds remained within of their nesting site throughout the year. It is capable of living near human activity, and one breeding site in Cameroon was located within of a maize plot. This and other recent observations suggest that the rockfowl has greater tolerance for degraded habitat than previously thought

relations.subject: 灰頸岩鶥
relations.evidence: This species is classified as vulnerable as its dwindling and fragmented populations are threatened by habitat destruction
relations.object_summary: 开发、采伐和围垦等活动导致适宜生境减少。

relations.subject: 灰頸岩鶥
relations.evidence: thumb|right|alt=A farmer in Cameroon makes his way up a hill, surrounded by his large-leafed crop.|A farmer's field on Mount Cameroon The grey-necked rockfowl is considered to be vulnerable due to habitat destruction, its isolated populations, collection of adults, predation, and a low breeding success rate. Its habitat is being destroyed to create large agricultural fields and cocoa plantations, as well as for logging and slash-and-burn agriculture. Due to the highly specialized requirements for its habitat, its population is very fragmented, and the species is believed to be naturally rare.{{cite journal | last = Harter| first = B
relations.object_summary: 原生林被砍伐导致栖息地面积和连通性下降。
